entry.title: SAP Cloud Connector stuck at "Preparation of tunnel certificate" while connecting BTP Trial
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am trying to connect SAP Cloud Connector to my SAP BTP Trial account.

Cloud Connector installation:
- Master (Primary Installation)
- Running on Windows

Issue:
While adding the subaccount, the status remains at:
"Preparation of tunnel certificate"

Earlier, I also received the message:
"We couldn't find any global accounts associated with your user"

Could anyone help me identify the cause?

Additional Information:
- Cloud Connector Version: 2.19.1
- BTP Trial Account : us10
- Internet connection is working

Thank you.

entry.title: Tax code determination in purchase order
entry.content:
HI

I want to determine tax code and tax rate for UK company code using standard tax determination procedure.

we don't want to use any customized configuration, so as per my knowledge we have created below steps but system is always capturing the V1 as the tax code in Purchase order.

  1. Access sequence- MWST
  2. Condition type - MWVS
  3. Tax procedure- 0TXGB
  4. Mapping of tax procedure to country
  5. maintaining condition master in FV11- below is standard condition master available for MWVS condition type and I'm having a doubt in this... ........for single country GB multiple tax codes are available and there is no differentiator to fetch the condition master then how system is only capturing V1 tax code.

 

I have also cross checked the below settings of determining the tax code in V1 in PO always

  1. Purchase info record
  2. Business Partner
  3. Last purchase order.

Can anyone please help me to understand this concept and solve the issue?

entry.title: Re: Calculation behind the Days Sales Outstanding and the...
entry.content:
Dear @Sakshi_Sharma51,

For Days Sales Outstanding, SAP documents the calculation for a specific period as:

DSO = ((total open receivables for last P1 months / P1) × 30) / (total monthly sales for last P2 months / P2)

where P1 is the rolling-average period for receivables and P2 is the rolling-average period for sales/revenue

More details below:

Days Sales Outstanding – SAP Help
Days Sales Outstanding – Calculation for a Specific Period
SAP KBA 3434600 – Days Sales Outstanding doesn’t show figures based on calendar year

For Days Payable Outstanding, SAP documents the direct/document-level calculation as:

DPO = SUM(invoice amount × days between invoice posting date and clearing date) / SUM(invoice amount)

SAP KBA 3757634 is relevant for reconciliation, because it explains that differences can occur if the manual calculation considers only RE invoice documents. Other relevant journal entry types, for example KR and migrated UE documents can also be included if they were cleared within the relevant period

More details below:

Calculation Logic: Days Payable Outstanding – SAP Help
SAP KBA 3757634 – Days Payable Outstanding Calculation Logic - Missing of Relevant Documents

Please also note that SAP provides a separate Days Payable Outstanding – Indirect Method, which uses an aggregate-level calculation based on rolling monthly averages for payables and purchases. Therefore for DPO the exact app/tile should be confirmed before comparing the SAP result with a manual calculation

Days Payable Outstanding – Indirect Method – SAP Help

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: S/4 migration, Custom CDS, compatibility views and re...
entry.content: So at risk of answering my own question - I was reading the following note https://me.sap.com/notes/2206980 where in section 1.3 and below it explains the circumstances that views will not work with the standard redirect and then it confirms for MKPF and MSEG for example, the views should be recoded to use the compatibility views (in other words the autoredirect will not work) similarly then for aggregated stock quantities. Because the CDS views are executed in the DB layer, they do not benefit from the redirect in these cases.

entry.title: Autonomous Diagnostics on Kyma Unplugged: Goose + LiteLLM + KRO + SAP AI Core in Production
entry.content:
Autonomous Cluster Diagnostics on Kyma Unplugged with Goose + LiteLLM + KRO + SAP AI Core in Production

As of 2026-07. Model versions and provider catalog reflect SAP AI Core availability at time of writing.

Modern Kubernetes operations need more than dashboards. They need reasoning.

This post describes a fully in-cluster autonomous diagnostic pipeline running on Kyma Unplugged:

  • a Goose AI agent with live kubectl and kyma CLI access
  • investigating anomalies adaptively
  • persisting structured findings to Kubernetes custom resources visible in Busola
  • streaming spend telemetry to SAP Cloud Logging via Kyma's built-in metric pipeline

No external calls at runtime. One terraform apply to deploy.

Stack

  • Kyma Unplugged (open-source Kyma; also runs on SAP BTP Kyma Runtime)
  • Goose AI — adaptive agent with shell tool access
  • LiteLLM proxy — routes to SAP AI Core (Claude Sonnet/Opus, GPT-5.5, Mistral, Nova via Bedrock)
  • Node.js llmproxy — bridges to SAP Orchestration V2 to enable prompt caching
  • KRO Resource Group Definitions — the entire deployment as a single declarative unit
  • Two parallel CronJobs — one static (v1, every 10 min), one adaptive (v2, daily)
  • Kyma Telemetry MetricPipeline → SAP Cloud Logging (OpenSearch) with a shipped dashboard

Custom resources produced

  • ClusterHealthReport — 12-section structured health analysis
  • AuditLogCatalog — security and configuration change audit
  • FleetStatus — aggregated fleet-wide health with live spend

Findings from production runs

  • 402 ingress gateway revisions accumulated in five days (unbounded revision history)
  • 18 leftover cluster-admin bindings from development tooling
  • An Istio EnvoyFilter silently blocking operator reconciliation for weeks
  • cert-manager ACME solver stuck for 5+ days on a DNS zone selector mismatch

Cost, approximate

  • ~$1.10 per adaptive v2 run
  • ~$0.004 per v1 static run when prompt caching is active on the Orchestration V2 path

Why an AI diagnostic pipeline?

Static probes tell you a pod is running. They don't tell you that an EnvoyFilter installed three weeks ago is silently intercepting reconciliation traffic. Or that cert-manager's ACME solver has a network policy gap waiting to surface at the next certificate renewal. Or that Istio has quietly accumulated 402 ingress gateway revisions in etcd since Tuesday.

Finding those needs correlation, inspection, and reasoning about blast radius — exactly what a language model with live tool access is built for.

So we put one inside the cluster. Not on someone's laptop, not as an ephemeral shell session — a scheduled, autonomous, in-cluster pipeline writing structured Kubernetes resources.

 

Architecture

The stack breaks into five layers:

  1. AI agent — Goose
  2. Gateway — OpenClaw + LiteLLM + llmproxy
  3. Backend — SAP AI Core
  4. Persistence — KRO CRDs + PostgreSQL
  5. Observability — Kyma Telemetry → SAP Cloud Logging

At a glance:

 

graph TD
  A[AI Agent<br/>Goose] --> G[Gateway<br/>OpenClaw · LiteLLM · llmproxy]
  G --> B[Backend<br/>SAP AI Core]
  A --> P[Persistence<br/>KRO CRDs · PostgreSQL]
  A --> O[Observability<br/>Kyma Telemetry → SAP Cloud Logging]

And in full:

 

graph TD
  subgraph Kyma Unplugged
    CJ1[CronJob: diagnose-v1<br/>every 10 min]
    CJ2[CronJob: diagnose-v2<br/>daily]

    subgraph diagnose-v2 Pod
      ORCH[Container: diagnose-v2<br/>orchestrator]
      GH[Container: goose-v2-health<br/>12-section analysis]
      GA[Container: goose-v2-audit<br/>security audit]
    end

    CJ1 -->|orch/ model prefix| LLMP[Node.js llmproxy :4000<br/>Orchestration V2 + cache_control]
    CJ2 --> ORCH
    ORCH -->|parallel exec| GH
    ORCH -->|parallel exec| GA

    GH -->|kubectl / kyma CLI| K8S[(Kubernetes API)]
    GA -->|kubectl / kyma CLI| K8S

    GH --> LLP[LiteLLM Proxy :4001]
    GA --> LLP

    LLMP -->|Orchestration V2| AICORE
    LLP -->|sap/ provider| AICORE[SAP AI Core<br/>Claude · GPT · Mistral · Nova]
    LLP -->|cost tracking| PG[(PostgreSQL)]
    LLMP -->|cost tracking| PG

    PUSHGW[Pushgateway] <-->|push accurate spend| CJ1
    LLP -->|/metrics<br/>pod annotations| KYMA_TEL[Kyma Telemetry<br/>metric-agent]
    PUSHGW -->|/metrics<br/>pod annotations| KYMA_TEL
    KYMA_TEL -->|MetricPipeline · OTLP| CLS[SAP Cloud Logging<br/>OpenSearch]

    GH -->|write| CHR[ClusterHealthReport CRD]
    GA -->|write| ALC[AuditLogCatalog CRD]

    OC[OpenClaw<br/>AI Gateway] --> LLP
    CHR -->|Busola extension| UI[Busola UI]
    ALC -->|Busola extension| UI
  end

Design principle: every component is a KRO Resource Group Definition. One terraform apply provisions the whole stack.

Two pipelines: static and adaptive

v1 — static (every 10 minutes). kyma alpha diagnose produces a ~51k-token cluster snapshot. Goose consumes it as a static blob and emits a structured summary. Fast, cheap, catches regressions between runs.

v2 — adaptive (daily). Goose gets direct kubectl and kyma access via the built-in developer extension and investigates across 12 sections:

# Section What Goose checks
1Health StatusCluster + Kyma module conditions
2IssuesDegraded resources, error events
3Kyma ModulesModule reconciliation state
4Impact AssessmentBlast radius of active issues
5Node ResourcesCPU/memory pressure, disk, conditions
6Workload HealthDeployments, statefulsets, daemonsets
7Security AssessmentRBAC surface, privileged workloads
8Drift DetectionResources diverging from declared state
9Cost AttributionNamespace resource consumption estimates
10Network Policy AuditConnectivity gaps, permissive policies
11Certificate Expiry ForecastCerts within 30 days of expiry
12Recent EventsHigh-frequency warning events in last hour

The v2 pod runs three containers concurrently — orchestrator, health worker, audit worker. Total job time is bounded by the longer worker (~230s health), roughly a third faster than running them sequentially.

LiteLLM proxy + SAP AI Core

The LiteLLM proxy runs as a Deployment on port 4001. The sap/ provider prefix routes to SAP AI Core using an OAuth2-obtained token, with the service key assembled from individual Kubernetes secret keys at startup.

What SAP AI Core actually is

SAP AI Core is not "OpenAI or Anthropic under a different URL." It is a proprietary orchestration layer — SAP's LLM Module — that fronts multiple backend providers:

 
OpenClaw / Goose v2
   │ openai-completions
   ▼
LiteLLM Proxy :4001
   │ sap/ provider + OAuth2 token
   ▼
SAP AI Core LLM Module
   ├──▶ AWS Bedrock (Claude Opus/Sonnet)
   ├──▶ Azure OpenAI (GPT-5.5, GPT-4o, GPT-4.1)
   └──▶ Mistral (mistral-large, mistral-small)

Behavioural notes from running this in production:

  • Reasoning traces don't come back on the foundation-model inference path. reasoning: true is accepted, but no reasoning_content appears in the response. SAP's LLM Module filters it server-side.
  • Vision works on all non-Mistral models. Images must be base64-encoded; public URLs are rejected.
  • drop_params: true is required. SAP AI Core rejects unknown parameters; this tells LiteLLM to drop them silently rather than fail. Do not turn it off.

Consequence of drop_params: true. Any parameter or beta header SAP doesn't recognize is silently dropped — including some Anthropic beta flags. "Accepted" doesn't mean "activated." If you rely on a beta feature (extended output, prompt caching, thinking blocks), assume it's off until you can see evidence in the response payload or in Postgres.

Production glue: the no_stream_on_tools hook

Three problems solved in a single LiteLLM pre-call hook:

  1. Disable streaming when tools are present — Claude Opus returns malformed streamed tool-call responses through SAP.
  2. Strip SAP Orchestration template placeholders from message content — they cause 400: Unused parameters: ['placeholder'] on the inference endpoint.
  3. Flatten pure-text content lists ([{"type":"text","text":"..."}]) to plain strings — the inference endpoint rejects them as 400: Unused parameters: ['text'].

Sketch of the content normalization:

 
def _normalize_content(content):
    if isinstance(content, str):
        return _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', content)
    if isinstance(content, list):
        all_text = all(
            isinstance(b, dict) and b.get('type') == 'text' for b in content
        )
        if all_text:
            return ' '.join(
                _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', b.get('text', '')) for b in content
            )
        # mixed content (e.g. vision): strip placeholders from text blocks only
        return [
            {**b, 'text': _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', b['text'])}
              if b.get('type') == 'text' else b
            for b in content
        ]
    return content
 
_SAP_TEMPLATE_RE = re.compile(r'\{\{\s*\?[^}]*\}\}')

That regex matches SAP's Orchestration placeholder syntax ({{?placeholder_name}}) that occasionally leaks into inference-path payloads.

Model tier aliases

Callers reference tiers, not specific models. Backing models can rotate centrally without touching every deployment:

  • reasoning-fast → SAP-hosted Claude Sonnet (default; used by both Goose v1 and v2)
  • reasoning-hard → SAP-hosted Claude Opus (deep reasoning; roughly an order of magnitude more expensive per call on our workload)

128k output on Claude

Configure max_tokens: 64000 together with extra_headers: {"anthropic-beta": "output-128k-2025-02-19"} to unlock 128k output on Claude. Goose v2 health reports regularly land above 17k tokens — well past the default 8k cap — which is circumstantial evidence the header is being honoured. Because drop_params: true can silently swallow beta headers SAP doesn't recognize, treat that as a working configuration to periodically re-verify rather than a settled guarantee.

SAP Orchestration V2 and prompt caching

The biggest cost lever we identified is prompt caching, and it lives on a different SAP AI Core API surface than the sap/ LiteLLM provider uses:

 

TypescenarioIdEndpointPayload
Foundation model inferencefoundation-models/v2/inference/deployments/{id}/chat/completionsOpenAI-compatible
Orchestrationorchestration/v2/inference/deployments/{id}/v2/completionSAP modules schema

The sap/ provider hits the foundation-model path. Prompt caching is designed to be exposed via Orchestration V2. To use it without rewriting every agent, we run a Node.js llmproxy next to LiteLLM:

 
Goose v1 (orch/ prefix)
   │
   ▼
Node.js llmproxy :4000
   │ detect orch/ prefix by deployment scenarioId
   │ convert OpenAI messages → V2 prompt_templating format
   │ inject cache_control: ephemeral on system + last user message
   │ strip cache_control from tools (SAP V2 rejects it there)
   │ extract final_result from V2 response
   ▼
SAP AI Core Orchestration V2

Implementation gotchas worth knowing:

  • scenarioId: "orchestration" on the deployment is the routing key — the only reliable way to distinguish orchestration from foundation-model deployments.
  • /v2 appears twice in the path: {AI_API_URL}/v2/inference/deployments/{id}/v2/completion.
  • cache_control on tools is rejected. Goose (correctly, per Anthropic spec) attaches cache_control at the top-level tool object; SAP's V2 schema rejects it there with 400: Additional properties not allowed. Strip it before forwarding.
  • Response wrapping. V2 returns final_result.choices, not top-level choices. Unwrap before returning to the caller.

Verifying caching is actually happening. Configuration alone isn't proof — SAP's LLM Module has a track record of reshaping payloads quietly. In our own smoke tests the llmproxy logs confirm routing via the V2 orchestration path with no cache_control errors, but cached_tokens: 0 came back on both calls of a short (~18-token) system prompt — too small to be a valid cache candidate. Verification at production scale (Goose v1's ~17k-token system prompt) is still on our to-do list. If caching matters for your budget, confirm by inspecting usage.cache_creation_input_tokens and usage.cache_read_input_tokens on V2 responses, or by watching the per-request cost delta in Postgres across repeat calls with a stable system prompt.

If the V2 path preserves caching end-to-end, v1's ~17k-token system prompt is written once and served from cache thereafter — the intended architecture behind the low per-call cost target for repeat v1 runs.

Observability: /metrics → Kyma Telemetry → SAP Cloud Logging

This is the piece that makes the whole stack operable, and it's cleaner than it has any right to be. No manual ServiceMonitor, no Prometheus deployment, no custom scrape config. Kyma Telemetry's metric-agent DaemonSet does the scraping natively via pod annotations, and a single MetricPipeline custom resource ships everything to SAP Cloud Logging over OTLP.

The pod-annotation contract

Any pod with these annotations gets scraped automatically:

 
metadata:
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port:   "4001"
    prometheus.io/path:   "/metrics"

That's the whole integration surface. Add the annotations to the LiteLLM Deployment and the Pushgateway; the metric agent picks them up on next reconciliation, scrapes /metrics on the specified port, wraps the samples as OTLP, and pushes them to the pipeline output.

The MetricPipeline resource

One CR describes where metrics go:

 
apiVersion: telemetry.kyma-project.io/v1beta1
kind: MetricPipeline
metadata:
  name: cloud-logging
spec:
  input:
    prometheus:
      enabled: true            # scrape annotated pods
  output:
    otlp:
      endpoint:
        valueFrom:
          secretKeyRef:
            name: sap-cloud-logging
            namespace: kyma-system
            key: ingest-otlp-endpoint

No agents to deploy, no scrape rules to maintain — Kyma Telemetry handles both scraping and shipping. Everything lands in SAP Cloud Logging's OpenSearch index metrics-otel-v1-*.

LiteLLM /metrics — the two config gotchas

Two settings in litellm_settings (not general_settings — the docs disagree with themselves; the correct section is litellm_settings😞

 
litellm_settings:
  callbacks: ["prometheus"]
  require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint: false

The first enables the Prometheus callback so /metrics is populated. The second removes auth from that endpoint so the metric agent can scrape it in-cluster without an API key.

Layered spend telemetry

The metric-agent path handles counters (rate-of-spend, latency, request counts) — great for trends but subject to reset on pod restart. For accurate absolute totals, a Pushgateway holds gauges that are refreshed from Postgres every 10 minutes by the v1 CronJob (litellm_actual_spend_usd, litellm_actual_tokens, litellm_actual_calls per model + cluster). The metric agent scrapes the Pushgateway with the same annotation pattern, so the pipeline is uniform.

Sources of truth:

  • Trend / real-time signal — LiteLLM counters via Kyma Telemetry
  • Accurate daily totals — Pushgateway gauges backed by Postgres
  • FleetStatus CRD — same accurate figures rendered in Busola per cluster
  • PostgreSQL — per-request detail; user-agent field distinguishes Goose (Goose/*) from OpenClaw interactive sessions (OpenAI/JS*)

The shipped OpenSearch dashboard

litellm-spend-dashboard.ndjson imports a ready-made dashboard into SAP Cloud Logging with 10 panels covering the operational questions you actually ask on Monday morning:

  • Per-model spend donut — where the money went
  • Spend over time, split by cluster — which cluster is bleeding
  • Goose vs interactive split — automation cost vs human cost
  • Latency by model — surface backend slowdowns
  • Token throughput and call volume — capacity signals
  • Cache-read vs cache-creation tokens (when caching is confirmed active) — how much prompt caching actually saved
  • Error rate by model — SAP-side hiccups, provider-side hiccups

Import once, and every cluster's telemetry shows up under the same views.

 

Cost and cadence

 The daily llm spend and cluster health overview is in busola landing page

 


ComponentModelApproximate costRun cadence
v1 static health (via Orchestration V2)orch/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet~$0.004 (target, with caching active)¹every 10 min (Goose every 12h)
v2 adaptive healthsap/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet~$1.10daily 03:07 UTC
v2 adaptive auditsap/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnetincluded abovedaily 03:07 UTC

Why the different prefixes? v1 uses orch/ because it routes through llmproxy for SAP Orchestration V2 (where prompt caching lives). v2 uses sap/ because it goes straight through LiteLLM. Same underlying model, different API surface — see the SAP Orchestration V2 and prompt caching section for the routing detail.

¹ v1 cost target assumes prompt caching is honored end-to-end on the Orchestration V2 path; production-scale verification is still pending — see the caching section.

Model routing. Goose v1 and v2 pin claude-4.5-sonnet directly via GOOSE_MODEL in the RGD (not through the reasoning-fast tier alias) so the diagnostic pipeline is independent of central tier rotation. OpenClaw interactive sessions default to reasoning-hard (Claude Opus). In the SAP Cloud Logging dashboard: sonnet traffic ≈ Goose diagnostics; opus traffic ≈ interactive user sessions. Per-model pricing and the authoritative model catalog live in SAP Note 3437766 (S-user login required).

Cadence evolution. v2 started at 30-minute intervals — around $150/day per cluster at that rate. Dropping to daily plus enabling caching on the v1 Orchestration V2 path is what turns the numbers around; we now run v1 Goose every 12 hours (gooseEvery=72) instead of once a month, and the total bill dropped substantially in observed Postgres totals.

GOOSE_MAX_TOKENS=16000 and GOOSE_CONTEXT_LIMIT=100000 cap per-response output and total context. With --max-turns 30, a typical v2 job completes in ~3 minutes.

Findings persist as Kubernetes CRDs with Busola custom extensions:

  • ClusterHealthReport — 12-panel detail view, CodeViewer per section
  • AuditLogCatalog — searchable tables of failed operations and config changes
  • FleetStatus — aggregated fleet card with live spend, AI Core catalog counts, and audit stats

Raw Goose working transcripts are archived to rolling ConfigMaps (goose-raw-<timestamp>, last 6 retained) for post-incident review.

 

Findings from production

Runs on development Kyma clusters surfaced issues static monitoring missed:

  • 402 ingress gateway revisions in 5 days. Istio revision history was unbounded; ReplicaSets accumulated and consumed etcd space. Root cause: a deployment with imagePullPolicy: Always cycling every 17 minutes.
  • 18 cluster-admin bindings. Leftover ClusterRoleBinding resources from dev tooling, still bound to unused service accounts. Goose ran kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=system:serviceaccount:default:dev-toolbox -A to confirm blast radius.
  • EnvoyFilter blocking reconciliation. An EnvoyFilter for SAP BTP Connectivity Proxy was intercepting traffic on a port the Istio operator uses. Reconciliation silently failed for weeks. Goose correlated the filter's selector labels against operator error messages to identify it.
  • cert-manager ACME solver gap. Three certificates stuck pending for 5+ days on a DNS zone selector mismatch in the ClusterIssuer. Goose produced the exact kubectl edit clusterissuer fix.

What's next

  • Reasoning traces. SAP's LLM Module strips Claude thinking blocks on the foundation-model path. When they're preserved (via Orchestration V2 or otherwise), OpenClaw's /reasoning toggle will surface chain-of-thought from in-cluster diagnostics.
  • Scoped remediation. Goose already has kubectl. Next: a tightly scoped set of approved remediation actions (unstuck finalizers, restart crashlooping pods) with every action written to AuditLogCatalog.
  • Cross-cluster comparison. Same pipeline across multiple Kyma instances, using spend telemetry to flag clusters with anomalous resource consumption for their workload profile.
  • On-call assistant. Raw transcript ConfigMaps already make past diagnostic runs searchable context. Next step is a Goose skill in OpenClaw's chat for ad-hoc cluster queries.

Full stack in a single KRO RGD. One terraform apply. No external runtime dependencies. That constraint — everything in-cluster, everything declarative, everything observable through Kyma's own pipeline — turned out to be the most consequential architectural decision of the whole system.

Stack: Goose AI · LiteLLM · SAP AI Core · KRO · Kyma Unplugged · SAP Cloud Logging · Busola · OpenClaw · PostgreSQL

Original idea, design and implementation by quovadis.

Co-written by Lulu and Ted, my AI assistants

 

Appendix: Configuration Snippets

The four configuration artefacts referenced throughout the post, consolidated for copy-paste. All <PLACEHOLDER> values are cluster-specific — substitute your own. Per-token pricing values are omitted from the snippets; see SAP Note 3437766 for the authoritative model catalog and current pricing (S-user login required).

A. LiteLLM config.yaml — SAP AI Core provider setup

 
model_list:
  # Anthropic via SAP AI Core (backed by AWS Bedrock upstream).
  # The double-dash separator is SAP's model-naming convention for grouped providers.
  - model_name: anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet   # backing model for Goose v2 diagnostic pipeline
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet
      max_tokens: 32000
    model_info:
      input_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_INPUT_COST>   # see SAP Note 3437766
      output_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_OUTPUT_COST>

  - model_name: anthropic--claude-4.7-opus     # used by OpenClaw interactive sessions
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/anthropic--claude-4.7-opus
      max_tokens: 64000
      extra_headers:
        anthropic-beta: "output-128k-2025-02-19"  # unlocks 128k output; verify it's honoured
    model_info:
      input_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_INPUT_COST>
      output_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_OUTPUT_COST>

  # Azure OpenAI via SAP AI Core
  - model_name: gpt-5.5
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/gpt-5.5
    model_info:
      input_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_INPUT_COST>
      output_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_OUTPUT_COST>

  # Tier aliases — callers reference these; backing model can rotate centrally
  - model_name: reasoning-fast                 # default for routine work
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet
      max_tokens: 64000

  - model_name: reasoning-hard                 # deep reasoning; ~9× cost of reasoning-fast
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/anthropic--claude-4.7-opus
      max_tokens: 64000
      extra_headers:
        anthropic-beta: "output-128k-2025-02-19"

litellm_settings:
  drop_params: true                            # mandatory — SAP AI Core rejects unknown params
  request_timeout: 600
  num_retries: 5
  retry_after: 10
  forward_client_headers_to_llm_api:
    - anthropic-version
  callbacks:
    - no_stream_on_tools.proxy_handler         # custom hook (see Appendix B)
    - prometheus                               # enables /metrics endpoint
  require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint: false     # allows in-cluster metric-agent scraping
                                               # MUST be under litellm_settings, not general_settings

general_settings:
  master_key: os.environ/PROXY_API_KEY         # injected from K8s Secret
  disable_spend_logs: false
  database_url: "postgresql://postgres:***@postgres-postgresql.<NAMESPACE>.svc.cluster.local:5432/litellm"

Environment variables. AICORE_AUTH_URL, AICORE_BASE_URL, AICORE_CLIENT_ID, AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET, AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP are injected at startup from individual Kubernetes Secret keys. The LiteLLM sap/ provider assembles the AI Core service key JSON automatically.

Note on the orch/ prefix. The orch/ model prefix used by Goose v1 does not live in this LiteLLM config — Goose v1's GOOSE_HOST points to the Node.js llmproxy (port 4000), which detects the orch/ prefix, converts the payload to SAP Orchestration V2 format, and calls SAP AI Core directly. See the "SAP Orchestration V2 and prompt caching" section for the routing diagram. sap/ traffic flows through LiteLLM as normal.

B. The no_stream_on_tools pre-call hook (full)

 
import re
from litellm.integrations.custom_logger import CustomLogger

# gpt-4o doesn't exhibit the malformed-streamed-tool-calls behaviour on SAP,
# and some upstream callers expect streaming to remain enabled for it.
STREAMING_EXEMPT_MODELS = {"gpt-4o"}

# Matches SAP Orchestration placeholder syntax like `{{?placeholder_name}}`
_SAP_TEMPLATE_RE = re.compile(r'\{\{\s*\?[^}]*\}\}')


def _normalize_content(content):
    """
    Fix two SAP AI Core inference-endpoint quirks:

    1. Strip `{{?...}}` Orchestration template placeholders — when these leak
       into the inference path they cause: 400 Unused parameters: ['placeholder']

    2. Flatten pure-text content lists to a plain string — the inference endpoint
       rejects [{"type":"text","text":"..."}] with: 400 Unused parameters: ['text']
       Mixed lists (vision blocks etc.) are left intact.
    """
    if isinstance(content, str):
        return _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', content)

    if isinstance(content, list):
        all_text = (
            len(content) > 0
            and all(
                isinstance(b, dict) and b.get("type") == "text"
                for b in content
            )
        )

        if all_text:
            return ' '.join(
                _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', b.get('text', ''))
                for b in content
            )
        # Mixed content (e.g. vision) — strip placeholders from text blocks only
        return [
            {**b, 'text': _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', b['text'])}
            if isinstance(b, dict) and b.get('type') == 'text' and 'text' in b
            else b
            for b in content
        ]

    return content


class NoStreamOnTools(CustomLogger):
    async def async_pre_call_hook(self, user_api_key_dict, cache, data, call_type):
        model = data.get("model", "") or ""
        tools = data.get("tools") or []

        # Disable streaming when tools are present — Claude Opus returns malformed
        # streamed tool-call responses through SAP's LLM Module
        if data.get("stream") and tools and model not in STREAMING_EXEMPT_MODELS:
            data["stream"] = False

        # Normalize all message content
        for msg in (data.get("messages") or []):
            if "content" in msg:
                msg["content"] = _normalize_content(msg["content"])

        return data


proxy_handler = NoStreamOnTools()

Mount this file into the LiteLLM container at /app/no_stream_on_tools.py and register it in litellm_settings.callbacks as shown in Appendix A.

C. Kyma Telemetry: pod annotations + MetricPipeline

LiteLLM Deployment pod-template annotations:

 
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port:   "4001"   # LiteLLM API + metrics port
        prometheus.io/path:   "/metrics"

Pushgateway Deployment pod-template annotations:

 
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port:   "9091"
        prometheus.io/path:   "/metrics"

MetricPipeline CR:

 
apiVersion: telemetry.kyma-project.io/v1beta1
kind: MetricPipeline
metadata:
  name: sap-cloud-logging
  namespace: kyma-system
spec:
  input:
    prometheus:
      enabled: true
      namespaces:
        include:
          - <NAMESPACE_WITH_LITELLM>   # e.g. default — where LiteLLM + Pushgateway run
          - <NAMESPACE_WITH_KYMA>      # e.g. kube-system — for Cilium / Hubble metrics
  output:
    otlp:
      endpoint:
        valueFrom:
          secretKeyRef:
            name: sap-cloud-logging    # created by the SAP Cloud Logging ServiceBinding
            namespace: kyma-system
            key: ingest-otlp-endpoint
      headers:
        - name: cluster
          value: <SHOOT_NAME>          # per-cluster attribution in OpenSearch
      tls:
        cert:
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: sap-cloud-logging
              namespace: kyma-system
              key: ingest-otlp-cert
        key:
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: sap-cloud-logging
              namespace: kyma-system
              key: ingest-otlp-key

The sap-cloud-logging Secret is provisioned via a ServiceBinding to a ServiceInstance referencing the central SAP Cloud Logging standard-plan instance. <SHOOT_NAME> is the Gardener shoot name (or any stable cluster identifier) — it becomes the cluster label in OpenSearch, enabling per-cluster filters and the fleet-wide breakdown in the shipped dashboard.

D. OpenClaw model routing config

The relevant section of openclaw.json showing the tier-alias pattern:

{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "model": {
        "primary": "litellm/reasoning-fast",
        "fallbacks": [
          "litellm/reasoning-hard",
          "litellm/gpt-5.5",
          "litellm/anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet",
          "litellm/gpt-4.1",
          "litellm/mistralai--mistral-large-instruct"
        ]
      },
      "pdfModel": {
        "primary": "litellm/reasoning-hard",
        "fallbacks": ["litellm/reasoning-fast", "litellm/gpt-4.1"]
      },
      "imageModel": {
        "primary": "litellm/reasoning-hard",
        "fallbacks": ["litellm/reasoning-fast", "litellm/gpt-4.1"]
      }
    }
  },
  "models": {
    "providers": {
      "litellm": {
        "baseUrl": "http://litellm-proxy.<NAMESPACE>.svc.cluster.local:4001",
        "apiKey": {"source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "LITELLM_API_KEY"},
        "api": "openai-completions",
        "models": [
          {
            "id": "anthropic--claude-4.7-opus",
            "name": "Claude 4.7 Opus via SAP AI Core",
            "maxTokens": 64000,
            "reasoning": true,
            "input": ["text", "image"],
            "cost": {"input": "<INPUT_COST>", "output": "<OUTPUT_COST>"}
          },
          {
            "id": "anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet",
            "name": "Claude 4.6 Sonnet via SAP AI Core",
            "maxTokens": 64000,
            "reasoning": true,
            "input": ["text", "image"],
            "cost": {"input": "<INPUT_COST>", "output": "<OUTPUT_COST>"}
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}

 

Routing summary. Interactive sessions default to reasoning-fast (Sonnet) for routine work. PDF and vision-heavy image tasks route to reasoning-hard (Opus) as the primary — the extra cost is worth it for accuracy on vision inputs. Callers only ever reference tier aliases, never underlying model IDs, so backing models can rotate centrally without touching agent configs.

Diagnostic pipeline exception. Goose v1 and v2 in the dev-toolbox RGD hard-pin orch/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet and sap/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet directly (not through the tier alias) so the diagnostic bill is stable and doesn't follow central tier rotation. This is deliberate.

References

KRO (Kube Resource Orchestrator)https://kro.run/
Kyma projecthttps://kyma-project.io/
Kyma Telemetry MetricPipelinehttps://kyma-project.io/external-content/telemetry-manager/docs/user/resources/05-metricpipeline.html
Kyma Telemetry Manager (repo)https://github.com/kyma-project/telemetry-manager
SAP AI Corehttps://help.sap.com/docs/sap-ai-core
SAP AI Core (Discovery Center)https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/serviceCatalog/sap-ai-core?region=all

entry.title: SAP QM - How to Perform Partial Reversal of Usage Decision Stock Posting?
entry.content:
I'm working on a custom Z program in SAP QM to support the partial reversal of a stock posting after Usage Decision (UD), and I'd like to understand the standard SAP approach for this requirement.

Example:

  • Inspection Lot Quantity: 100 EA
  • Usage Decision has been completed.
  • Entire 100 EA is posted from Quality Inspection Stock to Unrestricted Stock using Movement Type 321.
  • Later, it is identified that only 30 EA needs to be reversed due to a quality issue.

Expected Result:

  • Reverse only 30 EA from Unrestricted Stock back to Quality Inspection Stock using Movement Type 322.
  • The remaining 70 EA should continue to remain in Unrestricted Stock.
  • The Usage Decision should remain valid, and only the stock posting should be corrected.

I would appreciate your guidance on the following:

  1. Does SAP standard support partial reversal of a stock posting created during Usage Decision, or is only complete reversal supported?
  2. Which standard Function Module, BAPI, or API is recommended for performing a partial 321 → 322 reversal in a custom Z program?
  3. After posting the 322 movement, are there any standard QM Function Modules that should be called to keep the inspection lot quantities and system statuses consistent?
  4. Is there any standard process to update the remaining accepted quantity after the partial reversal?
  5. Are there any SAP Notes, documentation, or best practices related to this scenario?

I'm looking for a standard SAP-compliant solution and would like to avoid direct updates to QM application tables.

entry.title: Give Claude Code access to your SAP Cloud Logging
entry.content:
Introduction

Agents are only as good as the data we give them. We already give ours access to our code, business context, and Jira. So what about debugging and maintenance?

A year ago, when something went wrong, we opened our log dashboard and started searching. Find the error, read the stack trace, map it back to the code, form a hypothesis. That loop cost us hours every week. We do not do that anymore.

Giving your agent access to your logs changes the whole flow. It reads logs faster than any human. And when it also has your code, it does not need you for the chain: find the error, locate it in the code, and in the best case propose the fix.

Here is an example of what it looks like:

How to set it up

You can install the plugin to use the skill pretty easily. Just open Claude Code and run these two commands:

/plugin marketplace add https://github.tools.sap/I757089/sap-cloud-logs.git
/plugin install sap-cloud-logs@sap-cloud-logs

After running these two commands, you need to reload your plugins:

/reload-plugins

Before you can run the skill, you first need to generate a service key for the SAP Cloud Logging service in SAP BTP.

Open your SAP BTP subaccount and navigate to your SAP Cloud Logging service instance. In the Service Keys section, click Create. In the dialog that appears, enter a name for the service key and click Create.

Once the service key has been created, click its name to open the details dialog and view the credentials. Copy the JSON content and create a file named .sap-cloud-logs in your home directory.

In the file, assign a name to your environment (for example, "dev" for your development system) and paste the JSON credentials as its value:

{
  "dev": {
    "dashboards-endpoint": "https://dashboards-<id>.cls-XX.cloud.logs.services.euXX.hana.ondemand.com",
    "dashboards-username": "your-username",
    "dashboards-password": "your-password"
  }
}

Security note: .sap-cloud-logs contains plaintext credentials. Keep this file private — do not share it, commit it, or store it anywhere others can access.

You can now fetch your logs in Claude Code by simply invoking the skill:

/sap-cloud-logs:sap-cloud-logs Which services in the dev environment have had errors in the last 24 hours?

Next time something breaks. let the agent take the first pass. You might be surprised how far it gets.

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors als souveräner Cloud Service
entry.content:
SAP SuccessFactors als souveräner Cloud Service

Die digitale Souveränität wird für Unternehmen und Behörden zunehmend zu einem kritischen Faktor. Als souveräner Cloud Service angeboten, baut SAP SuccessFactors auf bewährten und kontinuierlich erweiterten Sicherheits-, Datenschutz- und Compliance-Merkmalen auf.

Zusätzlich kann die Bereitstellung von SuccessFactors auch relevante Anforderungen nach Souveränität unterstützen. SAP bietet damit eine innovative Antwort auf die wachsenden Anforderungen an erweiterte Compliance und operative Kontrolle im HR-Bereich.

Dieser Artikel möchte Ihnen einen Einblick in die Dimensionen der Souveränität und die damit einhergehenden Leistungsmerkmale von SAP SuccessFactors als souveränes Cloud Service Angebot geben!

Was bedeutet Souveränität in der Cloud?

Die dem souveränen SuccessFactors Service zu Grunde liegende Infrastructure-as-a-Service-Plattform von SAP wird mithilfe von Open-Source-Technologien entwickelt und betrieben.

Souveränität in Cloud-Services kann in vier Dimensionen abgebildet werden, die gemeinsam ein umfassendes Schutz- und Kontrollkonzept bilden.

Datensouveränität

  • Deutschland ist der Ort der Datenhaltung
  • Verarbeitung ausschließlich in Deutschland
  • Kundeneigene Verschlüsselung der Daten möglich incl. BYOK, HYOK
  • Umfassende Protokollierungsmöglichkeiten

Betriebliche Souveränität

  • Genehmigte Standorte der Leistungserbringung
  • EU- / deutsche Staatsbürgerschaft des operativen Personals
  • Sicherheitsüberprüftes Personal
  • Ergänzende Maßnahmen zum Sabotageschutz

Technische Souveränität

  • Gewährleistung physischer Sicherheit der Infrastruktur
  • BSI Grundschutzzertifikat für SAP Rechenzentren, siehe auch
    IT-Grundschutz zertifiziert deutsche SAP-Rechenzentren | SAP News
  • Software-Souveränität durch nicht-proprietäre Software-Basis
  • Hardware-Souveränität mittels Multivendor-Ansatz

Rechtliche Souveränität

  • Unternehmenssitz Deutschland
  • Gerichtsstand Deutschland
  • Schutz vor Einflussnahme fremdstaatlicher Stellen
  • Vertraglich gesicherte Prüfrechte

Erfahren Sie mehr unter SAP Sovereign Cloud für Ihre digitale Souveränität

SAP und BSI: Kooperation im Dienst der digitalen Souveränität!

Seit der Kooperationsvereinbarung von 2024 arbeiten SAP und das BSI gemeinsam daran, sichere Digitalisierung in konkrete Lösungen zu übersetzen. Als Global Player der Software- und Technologiebranche ist SAP ein entscheidender Faktor für die digitale Souveränität Deutschlands und Europas. Siehe auch

BSI und SAP: Partnerschaft für sichere Digitalisierung | SAP News Center

BSI und die Zukunft der digitalen Souveränität | SAP News Center

SAP SuccessFactors: Funktionale Exzellenz trifft auf Souveränität

SAP SuccessFactors als souveräner Cloud Service bietet die vollständige Bandbreite moderner HR-Funktionalitäten.

Core HR & Payroll

  • Vollständige Personalverwaltung mit souveräner Datenhaltung
  • Compliance-konforme Entgeltabrechnung nach deutschen Standards
  • Freigabe und Ausbau der SAP SuccessFactors EC Payroll für den Public Sector
  • Nahtlose Integration in bestehende SAP-Landschaften

Learning & Talent Management

  • KI-gestützte Talentidentifikation und -entwicklung
  • Personalisierte Lernpfade unter Wahrung der Datensouveränität
  • Umfassende Nachfolgeplanung und Karriereentwicklung

SAP KI-Anwendungsfälle

  • KI-Integration unter souveränen Bedingungen (Roadmap 2027)
  • Intelligente HR-Prozesse ohne Kompromisse bei der Datenkontrolle

Erfahren Sie mehr unter SAP Human Capital Management (HCM) sowie
auf unserer HCM Move Webseite mit dem Fokus Core HCM Prozesse zukunftsfähig zu gestalten

Fazit

SAP SuccessFactors als souveräner Cloud Service verbindet modernste HR-Technologie mit den höchsten Standards für Sicherheit, Datenschutz und operativer Kontrolle. Unternehmen und Behörden, die den Anspruch auf Souveränität haben, erhalten damit eine zukunftssichere Lösung, die sowohl funktionale Exzellenz als auch umfassende Souveränität gewährleistet.

Die Kombination aus technischer Innovation, rechtlicher Compliance und operativer Sicherheit macht die SAP SuccessFactors Sovereign Cloud Lösung zur idealen Wahl für Organisationen, die keine Kompromisse bei der Verarbeitung ihrer Daten eingehen möchten.

Weitergehende Informationen zur SAP SuccessFactors Cybersecurity finden Sie hier.

entry.title: How to use privileged Mode in a RAP Cross-BO Scenario for reading instance Features.
entry.content: We have created in SAP BTP ABAP Environment two RAP Business Objects with the following properties A Business Object ZAK_CommentTP o Having an authorization context NoCheckWhenPrivileged o with privileged mode disabling NoCheckWhenPrivileged; o The key field is called CommentId A Business Object ZAK_MainTP o enabled for Cross-BO Scenarios o source resolved, e.g. there is a field CommentId in the entity. What we want to achieve is that authorization checks are performed for reading, creating the comments in ZAK_CommentTP. However, if a user is authorized for an instance of ZAK_MainTP , then he can read and create comments in ZAK_CommentTP which refer to this instance without being explicitly authorized for the ZAK_CommentTP RAP BO. To achieve this, we have created actions and functions in the behavior of ZAK_MainTP , which then call the corresponding statements in ZAK_CommentTP in Privileged Mode. This works well for creating comments. Here, we have created an action createComment in ZAK_MainTP which uses the operation create-by-association for ZAK_CommentTP and the EML Statement MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZAK_CommentTP PRIVILEGED ENTITY CommentHeader CREATE BY \_CommentChild …. For Reading the Comments we have created a function which then uses Read Entities OF ZAK_CommentTP PRIVILEGED. Now, we would like to know when a comment can be deleted. Therefore, we can use the EML Statement GET PERMISSIONS PRIVILEGED ONLY INSTANCE FEATURES OF ZAK_CommentTP ENTITY …. to get the result of the feature control in ZAK_CommentTP . The result of this EML Statement is of type structure for permissions result ZAK_CommentTP . However, there is no possibility of creating a function in ZAK_MainTP which has this as result structure. So, I am searching for a way to create a function in the behavior of ZAK_MainTP whose parameters are the same as for the get_instance_features method in the ZAK_CommentTP .

entry.title: SAP Translation Hub Expands LLM-Based Translation to Five Indian Languages
entry.content:
Back in March, we announced the addition of seven languages to SAP Translation Hub: Armenian, Bengali, Georgian, Khmer, Lao, Maori, and Uzbek. Today, we're continuing that expansion with a set of five languages for India.

Gujarati, Kannada, Marathi, Tamil, and Telugu are now available through the LLM-based translation provider in both Document Translation and Software Translation on SAP BTP (regions EU10, EU11, and US10).

Collectively, these five languages are spoken by hundreds of millions of people across India, covering significant portions of the country. If your organization operates in India, serves Indian users, or is building SAP applications for an Indian market, you now have a direct path to translating your content into the languages your users actually work in.

To get started, use the SAP Translation Hub APIs or select LLM as your translation provider directly in the Document Translation or Software Translation user interface. The new languages will appear in your source and target language options.

As with all LLM-generated translations, we recommend reviewing the output before deployment to confirm it meets your quality expectations.

Learn More
- SAP Translation Hub Documentation
- LLM-based Translation Supported Languages
- Contact us: translationhub@sap.com

entry.title: How to use privileged Mode in a RAP Cross-BO Scenario for reading instance Features
entry.content:
Hi everybody, 

We have created in SAP BTP ABAP Environment two RAP Business Object with the following properties

  • A business Object ZAK_CommentTP
    • Having an authorization context NoCheckWhenPrivileged
    • with privileged mode disabling NoCheckWhenPrivileged;
    • The key field is called CommentId
  • A business Object ZAK_MainTP 
    • enabled for Cross-BO Scenarios source resolved, e.g. there is a field CommentId in the entity.

What we want to achieve is that authorization checks are performed for reading, creating the comments in ZAK_CommentTP.

However, if a user is authorized for an instance of  ZAK_MainTP , then he can read and create comments in ZAK_CommentTP which refer to this instance without being explicitly authorized for  the ZAK_CommentTP RAP BO.

To achieve this, we have created actions and functions in the behavior of ZAK_MainTP, which then call the corresponding EML statements in ZAK_CommentTP in Privileged Mode.

This works well for creating comments. Here, we have created an action  createComment in ZAK_MainTP  which uses the operation create-by-association for ZAK_CommentTP and the EML Statement

MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZAK_CommentTP PRIVILEGED

      ENTITY CommentHeader

       CREATE BY \_CommentChild ….

For Reading the Comments we have created a function which then uses Read Entities OF ZAK_CommentTP PRIVILEGED.

 

Now, we would like to know when a comment can be deleted. Therefore we can use the EML Statement  

    GET PERMISSIONS PRIVILEGED ONLY INSTANCE FEATURES OF ZAK_CommentTP        ENTITY ….

to get the result of the feature control in ZAK_CommentTP .      

The result of this EML Statement is of type structure for permissions result ZAK_CommentTP   .

However, there is no possibility of creating a function in ZAK_MainTP  which has this as result structure.        

 

So, I am searching for a way to create a function in the behavior for ZAK_MainTP whose  parameters are the same as for the  get_instance_features method in the ZAK_CommentTP  .

 

Any help and suggestions are welcome

entry.title: BPC Model to aDSO data flow to include data in yellow status request.
entry.content: We are trying get data from a BPC model to an aDSO through a DTP however we noticed that the data in the latest request in the BPC model, be it a DMP run, Data load, etc. which is in yellow status by default, is not being pushed into the aDSO. We need all the data in the model to be pushed to the aDSO. Please suggest a method to push all the data in the model to aDSO, including the data generated through the last request in the model. we are getting the data when the model is in Real time mode and, but the model needs to be in planning mode for data to be loaded and input. the DTP needs to be scheduled so all the data is periodically loaded into the aDSO. the request status cannot be manually every time as the DTP will be scheduled regularly.

entry.title: SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026 - Wave 1
entry.content:
Hi everyone, on behalf of the CX Product Success team at SAP, I'm delighted to share with you our latest deep dive videos as part of the SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026. 

Wave  #1 (April 2026)

1. Gainsight Customer Success Management for SAP Sales Cloud Version 2

 

Join Emma Falldorf, Product Manager SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 at SAP, for an exciting overview of the latest integrations between Gainsight Customer Success Management and SAP Sales Cloud Version 2. In this insightful video, we dive deep into the key benefits and usage scenarios of this powerful integration, provide a comprehensive look at how Gainsight enhances the customer experience.

The key highlights shared in this video are:

00:39 - Why did SAP invest in bringing Gainsight into SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?

2. Account Planning in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 in Action

Join Crystal Zhang, Product Manager SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 at SAP, for an exciting overview of the latest Account Planning capabilities of SAP Sales Cloud Version 2.
In this SAP Product Inspiration Video, learn why intelligent account planning is essential for modern sales teams and how SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 brings structure, insight, and focus to key account management. Through a concise end-to-end demo, the video shows how sales teams can capture customer goals and insights, define strategies and sales plans, monitor pipeline health, collaborate through action plans, and generate AI-powered executive summaries—all within one integrated solution. Discover how Intelligent CX from SAP helps turn account insights into consistent, measurable sales success.

3. AI Scouting in SAP Sports One

Join Michael Kesselmann, Product Manager SAP Sports One, and Achim Ittner, VP for Sports at SAP, for an exciting overview on embedded AI scouting capabilities in SAP Sports One. In this video, you will learn how clubs and federations can leverage Generative AI to streamline scouting workflows, accelerate decision-making, and create high‑quality player summaries with ease.
Discover how SAP Joule enhances the scouting process by consolidating extensive written reports, answering performance-related questions instantly, and generating structured management summaries that support faster and more informed squad planning decisions.

Watch this customer-led product inspiration video to learn why Mondi chose SAP Customer Experience solutions and how SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, SAP Commerce Cloud, and the SAP CX AI Toolkit are helping them modernize sales, service, and customer engagement.

entry.title: How to Implement SAP Change Document
entry.content:
Introduction:

If you have ever been asked the question — who changed that field, and when — you will immediately appreciate why change documents matter. In any serious SAP implementation, traceability is not a nice-to-have. It is a business requirement, often driven by audit, compliance, or simply the need to investigate data quality issues after the fact.

SAP provides a standard, reliable framework for exactly this purpose: Change Documents. Once set up, every create, update, and delete operation on your custom tables produces a detailed, timestamped record of exactly what changed, written automatically to two standard SAP tables — CDHDR and CDPOS. No custom logging table, no triggers, no background jobs. The framework handles it all.

In this blog series, we will walk through a complete implementation from scratch. We will use a realistic example — a customer master with header information and associated bank accounts — to show how change documents work in practice, and how to build a clean, reusable ABAP class that wraps both the database operations and the change document recording in a single, easy-to-call interface.

What are Change Documents?

Change Documents are SAP’s built-in audit trail mechanism for custom business objects. When correctly implemented, every time a record in your custom table is created, modified, or deleted, SAP automatically captures:

  • Who made the change — the SAP user name
  • When the change was made — date and time
  • Which transaction or program triggered the change
  • Which fields changed — at field level
  • What the old value was
  • What the new value is

This information is stored across two standard SAP tables that you never need to create or manage yourself.

How does the Framework Work?

The change document framework does not intercept your database calls automatically. You have to call it explicitly. The framework works as follows:
Everything starts with the creation of the Change Document Object. This can be done in ADT or in transaction SCDO. Here, you define a named Change Document Object and assign your custom tables to it. Whether the field change will be tracked or not depends on the flag set in the Data Element. In ADT, as soon as you activate the object, it generates a class with a method WRITE. If you are using SAP GUI transaction(SCDO), you have the option to generate either a class or a function module.

Your Responsibility

The generated class method (WRITE) does one thing only — it compares the old and new versions of your data that you pass to it, and writes the differences to CDHDR and CDPOS. It does not touch your Z tables at all. That means your code is responsible for two things in the correct order:

  1. Write to your Z tables using INSERT, MODIFY, or DELETE
  2. Call the generated class method/FM, passing it both the old and new versions of the data

In the case of a Managed RAP application, the call is handled automatically by the framework. We do have to specify the Change Document Object in the Behaviour Definition.

Both operations must happen within the same Logical Unit of Work, so they commit together. If the database update succeeds but the FM call fails, you want a rollback – not a situation where your data changed, but no change document was recorded.

This is the most important point to understand. The method cannot read old values from the database itself — by the time you call it, your MODIFY statement may have already overwritten the old data. You must read the old values from the database before making any changes, hold them in memory, and pass them to the FM alongside the new values.

In the case of a Managed RAP application, the call is handled automatically by the framework. We do have to specify the Change Document Object in the Behaviour Definition.

Tables

We are going to work with two tables ZZCUSTOMER and ZZCUSTOMER_BANK. This is a typical header item table. I am going to work with UUID as key as I will extend these to the RAP application later. I have used SAP Data Elements for fields. These Data Elements for fields NAME, STREET, CITY, POSTAL_CODE, COUNTRY etc. have ‘Change Document Logging’ checked.

@EndUserText.label : 'Customer'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zzcustomer {
 
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key customer_id       : sysuuid_x16 not null;
  name                  : ad_name1;
  street                : ad_street;
  city                  : ad_city1;
  postal_code           : ad_pstcd1;
  country               : land1;
  local_created_by      : abp_creation_user;
  local_created_at      : abp_creation_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_by : abp_locinst_lastchange_user;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
}
@EndUserText.label : 'Customer Bank Details'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zzcustomer_bank {
 
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key bankid            : sysuuid_x16 not null;
  customer_id           : sysuuid_x16;
  bank_country          : banks not null;
  bank_key              : bankk not null;
  bank_account          : bankn;
  currency              : waers;
  local_created_by      : abp_creation_user;
  local_created_at      : abp_creation_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_by : abp_locinst_lastchange_user;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

Change Document Object

Create Change Document Object in ADT or in transaction SCDO (I’ve used ADT). Add tables ZZCUSTOMER and ZZCUSTOMER_BANK. Check ‘Log Multiple Changes’ in both tables. Activate. You will notice it will create a class (ZCL_ZZCUSTOMER_CHDO) and show it just under the Change Document.

Wrapper Class

This class acts as a combined repository and audit layer — all database INSERT, MODIFY, DELETE operations on ZZCUSTOMER and ZZCUSTOMER_BANK go through this class, which also records the change document automatically. The caller simply calls the method and then COMMIT WORK.

Definition

CLASS zcl_customer_change_wrapper DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .
 
  PUBLIC SECTION.
 
    METHODS create
      IMPORTING
        is_hdr TYPE zzcustomer
        it_dtl TYPE zzcustomerbank_t
      RAISING
        cx_sy_open_sql_error.
 
    METHODS update
      IMPORTING
        is_hdr TYPE zzcustomer
        it_dtl TYPE zzcustomerbank_t
      RAISING
        cx_sy_open_sql_error.
 
    METHODS delete
      IMPORTING
        iv_customer_id TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id
      RAISING
        cx_sy_open_sql_error.
 
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
 
    METHODS call_write_document
      IMPORTING
        iv_customer_id          TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id
        object_change_indicator TYPE if_chdo_object_tools_rel=>ty_cdchngindh
        it_hdr_new              TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer
        it_hdr_old              TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer OPTIONAL
        it_dtl_new              TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank
        it_dtl_old              TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank OPTIONAL.
 
 
    METHODS read_current_customer
      IMPORTING
        iv_customer_id TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rs_hdr)  TYPE zzcustomer.
 
    METHODS read_current_customer_bank
      IMPORTING
        iv_customer_id TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rt_dtl)  TYPE zzcustomerbank_t.
ENDCLASS.

Class Implementation

Method CREATE

METHOD create.
  SELECT SINGLE customer_id FROM zzcustomer
      WHERE customer_id = @IS_hdr-customer_id
      INTO @DATA(lv_exists).
 
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_sy_open_sql_error.
  ENDIF.
 
  DATA it_hdr_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
 
  APPEND INITIAL LINE TO it_hdr_new ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<chgdoc_hdr>).
  MOVE-CORRESPONDING is_hdr TO <chgdoc_hdr> .
  <chgdoc_hdr>-kz = 'I' .
 
  LOOP AT it_dtl ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<new_record>) .
    APPEND INITIAL LINE TO it_dtl_new ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<chgdoc_dtl>) .
    MOVE-CORRESPONDING <new_record> TO <chgdoc_dtl> .
    <chgdoc_dtl>-kz = 'I' .
  ENDLOOP.
 
 
  INSERT zzcustomer FROM @IS_hdr.
  INSERT zzcustomer_bank FROM TABLE @IT_dtl.
 
  " Change doc
  call_write_document(
    object_change_indicator = 'U'
    iv_customer_id          = is_hdr-customer_id
    it_hdr_new              = it_hdr_new
    it_dtl_new              = it_dtl_new ).
 
ENDMETHOD.

The object_change_indicator was passed value ‘U’ instead of ‘I’ to align with how the change document works in RAP.

Method UPDATE

METHOD update.
 
  " Read old state from DB before modifying
  DATA(ls_hdr_old) = read_current_customer( is_hdr-customer_id ).
  DATA(lt_dtl_old) = read_current_customer_bank( is_hdr-customer_id ).
 
  DATA it_hdr_old TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_old TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
  DATA it_hdr_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
 
  APPEND ls_hdr_old TO it_hdr_old .
  APPEND LINES OF lt_dtl_old TO it_dtl_old .
 
  APPEND INITIAL LINE TO it_hdr_new ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<chgdoc_hdr>).
  MOVE-CORRESPONDING is_hdr TO <chgdoc_hdr> .
  <chgdoc_hdr>-kz = 'U' .
  <chgdoc_hdr>-client = sy-mandt .
 
  LOOP AT it_dtl ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<new_record>) .
    APPEND INITIAL LINE TO it_dtl_new ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<chgdoc_dtl>) .
    MOVE-CORRESPONDING <new_record> TO <chgdoc_dtl> .
    <chgdoc_dtl>-kz = 'U' .
    <chgdoc_dtl>-client = sy-mandt .
  ENDLOOP.
 
  MODIFY zzcustomer FROM @IS_hdr.
  MODIFY zzcustomer_bank FROM TABLE @IT_dtl.
 
  " Change doc
  call_write_document(
    object_change_indicator = 'U'
    iv_customer_id          = is_hdr-customer_id
    it_hdr_new              = it_hdr_new
    it_hdr_old              = it_hdr_old
    it_dtl_new              = it_dtl_new
    it_dtl_old              = it_dtl_old  ).
 
ENDMETHOD.

Method DELETE

METHOD delete.
 
  "Read old state from DB before modifying
  DATA(ls_hdr_old) = read_current_customer( iv_customer_id ).
  DATA(lt_dtl_old) = read_current_customer_bank( iv_customer_id ).
 
  DATA it_hdr_old TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_old TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
  DATA it_hdr_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer .
  DATA it_dtl_new TYPE zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>tt_zzcustomer_bank .
 
  APPEND ls_hdr_old TO it_hdr_old .
  APPEND LINES OF lt_dtl_old TO it_dtl_old .
 
  "Delete detail first, then header (referential integrity)
  DELETE FROM zzcustomer WHERE customer_id = @iv_customer_id.
  DELETE FROM zzcustomer_bank WHERE customer_id = @iv_customer_id.
 
  " Change doc
  call_write_document(
    object_change_indicator = 'D'
    iv_customer_id          = iv_customer_id
    it_hdr_new              = it_hdr_new
    it_hdr_old              = it_hdr_old
    it_dtl_new              = it_dtl_new
    it_dtl_old              = it_dtl_old  ).
 
ENDMETHOD.

Private helpera methods CALL_WRITE_DOCUMENT, READ_CURRENT_CUSTOMER, READ_CURRENT_CUSTOMER_BANK

METHOD call_write_document.
  TRY .
      zcl_zzcustomer_chdo=>write(
        EXPORTING
                      objectid                = CONV cdobjectv( iv_customer_id )
                      tcode                   = sy-tcode
                      utime                   = sy-uzeit
                      udate                   = sy-datum
                      username                = sy-uname
                      object_change_indicator = object_change_indicator
                      xzzcustomer             = it_hdr_new
                      yzzcustomer             = it_hdr_old
                      upd_zzcustomer          = 'U'
                      xzzcustomer_bank        = it_dtl_new
                      yzzcustomer_bank        = it_dtl_old
                      upd_zzcustomer_bank     = 'U'
        IMPORTING
                      changenumber            = DATA(change_number)
      ).
 
    CATCH cx_chdo_write_error.
  ENDTRY.
ENDMETHOD.
 
 
METHOD read_current_customer_bank.
  SELECT * FROM zzcustomer_bank
    WHERE customer_id = @iv_customer_id
    INTO TABLE @RT_dtl.
ENDMETHOD.
 
METHOD read_current_customer.
  SELECT SINGLE * FROM zzcustomer
    WHERE customer_id = @iv_customer_id
    INTO @RS_hdr.
ENDMETHOD.

Usage Example

Testing: Create a New Customer

DATA: current_time TYPE timestampl,
      customer_id  TYPE zzcustomer-customer_id,
      bank_id      TYPE zzcustomer_bank-bankid.
 
GET TIME STAMP FIELD current_time.
"Get UUIDs for keys
customer_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).
bank_id     = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).
 
DATA(lo_cust) = NEW zcl_customer_change_wrapper( ).
 
lo_cust->create(
            is_hdr = VALUE zzcustomer(
                              customer_id           = customer_id
                              name                  = 'ABC Company'
                              street                = 'Long Street'
                              city                  = 'London'
                              postal_code           = 'EC10 5GH'
                              country               = 'GB'
                              local_created_by      = sy-uname
                              local_created_at      = current_time
                              local_last_changed_by = sy-uname
                              local_last_changed_at = current_time
                              last_changed_at       = current_time
                           )
            it_dtl = VALUE zzcustomerbank_t(
                                ( bankid = bank_id
                                  customer_id           = customer_id
                                  bank_country          = 'GB'
                                  bank_key              = '123456'
                                  bank_account          = '123456789'
                                  currency              = 'GBP'
                                  local_created_by      = sy-uname
                                  local_created_at      = current_time
                                  local_last_changed_by = sy-uname
                                  local_last_changed_at = current_time
                                  last_changed_at       = current_time
                            )
         )
  ).
 
COMMIT WORK.

Check table ZZCUSTOMER, ZZCUSTOMER_BANK, CDHDR and CDPOS.

Testing: Update Customer

DATA: current_time TYPE timestampl.
 
GET TIME STAMP FIELD current_time.
 
DATA(lo_cust) = NEW zcl_customer_change_wrapper( ).
 
  "Get customer_id and bank_id UUIDs from table ZZCUSTOMER and ZZCUSTOMER_BANK
lo_cust->update(
        is_hdr = VALUE zzcustomer(
                          customer_id = '005056A3C6531FD18AECAF7C7E10D978'
                          name                  = 'XYZ Company'        "<< Change
                          street                = 'New Long Street'    "<< Change
                          city                  = 'London'
                          postal_code           = 'EC10 5GH'
                          country               = 'GB'
                          local_created_by      = sy-uname
                          local_created_at      = current_time
                          local_last_changed_by = sy-uname
                          local_last_changed_at = current_time
                          last_changed_at       = current_time
                        )
        it_dtl = VALUE #(
                            ( bankid                = '005056A3C6531FD18AECAF7C7E10F978'
                              customer_id           = '005056A3C6531FD18AECAF7C7E10D978'
                              bank_country          = 'GB'
                              bank_key              = '30-40-50'   "<< Change
                              bank_account          = '123456789'
                              currency              = 'GBP'
                              local_created_by      = sy-uname
                              local_created_at      = current_time
                              local_last_changed_by = sy-uname
                              local_last_changed_at = current_time
                              last_changed_at       = current_time
                            )
                         )
 ).
 
COMMIT WORK.

Check table ZZCUSTOMER, ZZCUSTOMER_BANK, CDHDR and CDPOS.

Testing: Delete Customer

DATA(lo_cust) = NEW zcl_customer_change_wrapper( ).
 
"Get customer_id UUID from table ZZCUSTOMER
lo_cust->delete( iv_customer_id =  '005056A3C6531FD18AECAF7C7E10D978' ) .
COMMIT WORK.

Check table ZZCUSTOMER, ZZCUSTOMER_BANK, CDHDR and CDPOS.

Conclusion

Implementing a reliable audit trail doesn't require complex custom logging logic or performance-heavy database triggers. As shown in this guide, leveraging SAP’s standard Change Document Framework (SCDO) gives you a robust, compliant, and optimized solution right out of the box.

By wrapping both the database persistence layers (INSERT, MODIFY, DELETE) and the tracking mechanisms inside a single ABAP wrapper class, we successfully encapsulate the business logic. This pattern ensures that:

  • Data consistency is maintained via atomic operations (both the Z-tables and the CD tables update within the same Logical Unit of Work).

  • Code reusability is enhanced, hiding the boilerplate logic of tracking old vs. new memory states from caller programs.

  • Future readiness is built-in, paving a clean path if you decide to extend these custom tables into a Managed RAP (RESTful Application Programming) architecture down the line.

With CDHDR and CDPOS tracking your field-level changes seamlessly behind the scenes, answering "who changed what, and when" becomes a trivial task rather than a development headache.

entry.title: Re: Building a Seamless Onboarding Experience for Global Assignees
entry.content:
Hi @sireeshpatil  @Gopinath_Murugaiyan ,

After onboarding is initiated, we can see two records for the same employee in the Onboarding Dashboard:

  1. Home Profile – Onboarding record
  2. Host Profile (Global Assignment) – Onboarding record

Question: Is it expected standard behavior that both the Home and Host profiles are displayed as separate records in the Onboarding Dashboard when a Global Assignment onboarding process is initiated?

Or could this indicate:

  • A configuration issue,
  • A duplicate onboarding process,
  • Or an incorrect onboarding trigger/business rule setup?

If this is expected behavior, could you please share the SAP design rationale or documentation reference explaining why two onboarding records are displayed?

Thank you in advance for your guidance.

entry.title: SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026 - Wave 2
entry.content:
Hi everyone, on behalf of the CX Product Success team at SAP, I'm delighted to share with you our latest deep dive videos as part of the SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026. 

Wave #2 (May-June 2026)

 
1. Innovate and Grow your Business with SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition
 
 

Join Daniela Stehle (Solution Value Advisor for Customer Experience at SAP) in an insightful discussion with Lucas Timm from SAP Product Management about the next evolution of digital commerce. Watch this product inspiration video to learn why SAP launched SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition and how it enables fast time‑to‑value for companies looking to quickly establish or expand their digital sales channels.
Discover the key differentiators of this integrated solution, including seamless end‑to‑end processes between commerce and SAP S/4HANA Cloud, reduced complexity, and a business‑user‑friendly experience. Learn how customers benefit from faster innovation, lower total cost of ownership, and scalable growth—while SAP manages the technical foundation.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:49 - Why did SAP release this new SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition?
01:38 - What are the key differentiators of this new solution?
02:39 - Which benefits do Commerce key users gain?
03:05 - What’s the business value for existing SAP customers?
05:12 - Start of the Demo
05:25 - Demo: Consumer-grade B2B experiences
06:37 - Demo: Activate your storefront from ERP data
07:48 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

2. Connected E-Commerce across SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition

Join Daniela Stehle (Solution Value Advisor for Customer Experience at SAP) in an insightful discussion with Lucas Timm from SAP Product Management about the next step in connected enterprise commerce. Watch this product inspiration video to explore how SAP‑managed integration seamlessly connects SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition—eliminating complexity and enabling truly unified end‑to‑end business processes.
Learn why integration has been a long‑standing challenge in commerce landscapes and how SAP’s managed approach removes the need for custom middleware, allowing customers to focus on selling, order management, and delivering great experiences while SAP handles connectivity in the background.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Start
00:26 - Introduction
00:49 - Why is integration so important between E-Commerce and ERP?
01:34 - How is SAP-managed integration different from customer-managed integration?
02:31 - What does SAP-managed integration mean for SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition?
03:14 - What’s the business value of SAP-managed integration for SAP Commerce Cloud?
04:09 - Start of the Demo
04:24 - Demo: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public - Sales Order Management
04:36 - Demo: SAP for Me - Activation of SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition
05:25 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Stores and configure Countries, Regions, Languages and Currencies
06:10 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Product Sales Catalogs, Content Catalogs and Digital Sales Channels
07:09 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Customers and Business Partners
07:25 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Price Visibility and Taxes
07:37 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Order Management and Fulfillment, Sourcing Strategies and Markets
08:07 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Payment Options
08:21 - Demo: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public - Product Maintenance
10:12 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Product Enrichment using Business AI
12:21 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Catalog Management & Assortment
13:41 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Storefront - Experience Shopping
14:14 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

3. SAP Engagement Cloud: AI Innovations and Google Cloud Partnership across the SAP Autonomous Suite

Join Nisha and Valentin from SAP for a strategic look at how SAP Engagement Cloud is redefining customer engagement in the era of Agentic AI and the SAP Autonomous Suite.
Discover how SAP Engagement Cloud extends beyond traditional marketing to transform enterprise-wide business signals into contextual, relevant, and timely customer interactions. Learn how SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Joule, and the expanded partnership with Google Cloud and Gemini Enterprise combine to create a powerful multi-agent framework that helps organizations deliver more intelligent and personalized engagement across marketing, commerce, sales, service, and operations.
In the inspiration demo, see how AI assistants and autonomous agents help marketers build data-driven campaigns, personalize content, optimize customer journeys, and make real-time decisions based on inventory, customer behavior, geographic data, and business signals. Learn how SAP Engagement Cloud enables organizations to move from isolated marketing activities to business-scale engagement, delivering relevance, agility, and competitive differentiation across the entire customer lifecycle.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:50 - How does SAP Engagement Cloud fit into the SAP Autonomous Enterprise strategy?
01:51 - How does SAP think about customer data and Agentic AI to elevate engagements?
04:32 - How can engagement become a competitive differentiator for a company?
05:55 - Demo: SAP Engagement Cloud and Google Gemini
07:27 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

Back to main page.
